facts_disclosed: Night shifts destroying health — can't sleep, weight gain

turns.content: I think I need to make some serious changes in my life because my body is literally falling apart
That sounds concerning. What's going on — can you tell me more about what you're experiencing?

ok so I haven't slept more than 4 hours in like a week and a half. I'm not exaggerating. I get home from my shift and I'm exhausted but my body just will NOT shut down. I lie there staring at the ceiling for hours and then when I finally fall asleep my alarm goes off and it starts all over again

That sounds really rough. Chronic sleep deprivation like that affects literally everything — your mood, your immune system, your ability to think clearly. Is something specific keeping you up, like stress or anxiety? Or does it feel more like your body clock is just completely off?

working overnights is wrecking me honestly. like my body is falling apart from it. my body has no idea what time zone it's in anymore. I come home at 7:30am and the whole world is awake and loud and bright and my body is like oh we're supposed to be awake now?? and then at 3am at work when I should be alert I'm barely keeping my eyes open

That circadian rhythm disruption is one of the biggest health risks for night shift workers — it's well documented. Your body's internal clock is constantly being pulled in two directions. How long have you been on nights at this point?

almost two years now. and like it's not just the sleep thing either. I've gained back all the weight I lost. like ALL of it. I was doing so well and now I'm stress eating at 2am in the break room because my body thinks it needs fuel but also it's the middle of the night so everything I eat just sits there. I can literally feel the inflammation in my body

That's really frustrating, especially when you'd made progress and now feel like you've backslid. Night shift weight gain is incredibly common though — your metabolism, hunger hormones, everything gets disrupted. The 2am eating is your body's cortisol response going haywire because it's confused about when it's supposed to be active. Have you talked to your doctor about any of this?

I KNOW I should go to the doctor but when am I supposed to go?? I work all night and sleep all day and doctors offices are open during my sleep time. it's like the schedule makes it impossible to take care of yourself which is so ironic because I literally work in healthcare

The irony of healthcare workers not being able to access healthcare is real and awful. But this is important enough to prioritize — could you schedule an appointment on a day off, even if it means losing some sleep? A lot of clinics also have Saturday hours now. Your body is telling you something pretty loudly and it's worth getting checked out, especially the sleep issues.

yeah you're right. and honestly my anxiety has been worse too like significantly worse. I get these moments at work where my heart is racing and I can't tell if it's because something is actually wrong or if my body is just freaking out from exhaustion. that's scary when you're responsible for sick kids

Okay, that's a really important thing you just said. Anxiety spikes combined with severe sleep deprivation and the physical symptoms you're describing — that's your body in crisis mode. And you're right that it's especially concerning given your job. The last thing you want is your judgment or reaction time being affected when you're caring for patients. Have you thought about whether switching to day shifts might be an option?

there's a waitlist for days at my unit and it's long. like really long because obviously everyone wants days. I could transfer to a different unit but I love my patients and my team and I don't want to start over somewhere new just because of the schedule you know?

I understand that — your team and patients matter to you, and starting over is daunting. But I want to gently push back: if the schedule is destroying your sleep, causing weight gain, worsening your anxiety, and potentially affecting your ability to do your job safely, then something has to give. Could you talk to your manager about getting moved up on the waitlist given the health impact? Sometimes if you frame it as a medical necessity, there's more flexibility.

I didn't think about framing it as a medical thing. my charge nurse is actually pretty understanding so maybe if I got a doctor's note or something... ugh I just hate that it's come to this. I feel like I should be able to handle it but clearly I can't and that makes me feel weak

You are not weak. You've been working against your own biology for almost two years — that takes incredible strength, honestly. Recognizing that it's not sustainable isn't weakness, it's wisdom. Plenty of people burn out completely before they ever get to the point of saying "I need to change this." Getting a doctor involved is a smart, proactive move, not a defeat.

ok you're right. I'm going to call my doctor's office tomorrow and see if they have any Saturday appointments and I'm going to talk to my charge nurse about the day shift waitlist. I just need to actually DO it instead of just thinking about it at 4am when everything feels hopeless lol

Yes — make those calls tomorrow, don't wait. Write it down right now so you don't forget when you wake up. And for what it's worth, 4am is when everything feels hopeless for night shift workers because your cortisol is at its lowest. Things genuinely look different in the daylight. You're making the right call.

writing it in my notes app right now. "call dr" and "talk to jessica about day shift." ok. I feel like this is the start of something. or the start of admitting something I've been ignoring for a while. either way thank you for not letting me just brush it off

That's a really brave step. You deserve to feel healthy and rested — that's not a luxury, it's a basic need. Let those calls be the first domino. Take care of yourself, Elena. You spend all night taking care of everyone else's kids; it's okay to put yourself first for once.
